the homesteader has to pay now"
Now If l were a man, I'd" be-
gan Jane positively.


you don't have to be man to
get government land,' replied the
Man from Omaha" Then he told
her all about the Dakota reserva.
ton openings and how to get a
claim there.


An idea was born there. 1t grew
and developed. 1t called for Jane
with promised reward which far
exceeded her air castles of former
days. Here was something worth
fighting for, planning for, some
thing substantial and real-land,
her land, her home, her future
Nights were spent in discussing
the idea with mother; for hours
they bent their heads over maps
and glowing descriptions of the
new country. She drew nearer the
idea when she again spent her va
cation In the west; this time it
meant trip to Aberdeen, S. D.,
! where she became one of a hun
dred thousand applicants for gov
eminent land.


Again there was disappointment
and hope was almost shattered, for
there were 10 applicants for every
winning ticket to- be drawn in the
governments land lottery. The
journey back to the "old country"
was beclouded with the fear of dis
appointment.


Then one day the sunshine all
came back She had won. All win
ter long she lived two liyes-the
school teacher and the woman
farmer-in the same person.
The next summer followed and
then the packing and the leave
takings of old friepds and old GUS
toms. They started for the new
country high in hope and with a
few necessary household furnish
ings, plain. warm clothes, and a
bare handful of money, represent.
Ing the savings of 10-year toll
on the school rOstrum.


On the night of October 81 they
arrived in Aberdeen and early next
morning Jane poined the line of
applicants at the land office, each
intent upon getting an early choice
of land, and along in the afternoon
she selected her farm" she picked
out a claim next to one chosen by
the man in the line before her. He
looked like farmer and Jane con-
strued this to be a valuable asset
for herself; she would have some
one to go to for advice.


Hastily tripping down the steps
with radiant face and dancing
eyes, Jane didn't even see the
span of mules until sudden
"Whoa!" prevented the prides of
Missouri from being run over by
the thoughtless young woman.


Excuse my driving. lady. Il
well, If it ain't-what in the world
are you doing here?" And the
Man from Omaha" jumped from
the wagon seat.


Continued tomorrow
